topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the dimly lit bar felt thick, clinging to my skin like damp velvet. I nursed my lukewarm whiskey, my gaze fixed on the stage where Elara was now pouring her voice into the melody. Every note she hit was a precise, glittering shard, and as the familiar chord progression unfolded, a cold knot tightened beneath my ribs. My fingers, resting on the worn wood of the bar, began to drum a silent, uneven rhythm against my thigh. I watched her, cataloging every subtle shift in her posture, searching for a flaw, a deviation from the practiced perfection that made my own composition feel so fragile. When the final, soaring note faded, leaving a ringing silence, I couldn't move. My throat felt constricted, a dry, tight band around my vocal cords, and I slowly pushed myself up, the movement stiff and deliberate, needing distance before I could face the sudden, hollow echo of her performance.

[story 2]

I stood near the back exit, pretending to examine a chipped paint sample on the wall, but my attention was entirely snagged on the band. Liam’s rendition of ‘Echo Bloom’ was flawless, too flawless. The way he held the microphone, the effortless swing of his head—it was all so smooth, so controlled. A sharp prickle ran down my spine, making the fine hairs on my arms stand up. I gripped my empty glass so hard my knuckles bleached white. *That* was my music, the intricate sorrow I’d poured into those harmonies, and hearing it spun out by someone else felt like watching a delicate mechanism being dismantled. I took a shallow breath, trying to regulate the quickening rhythm of my pulse. I needed to leave, needed the anonymity of the street noise, before the sheer weight of that sonic recognition crushed me.

[story 3]

My hands were shaking so badly I had to press them flat against my jeans pockets. From across the room, the sound washed over me—the unmistakable melody of my latest piece, played with an unfamiliar, yet deeply familiar, passion. It hit me like a physical blow. I couldn't look away. I watched the performer, their movements fluid and assured, inhabiting the space with a confidence that made my own carefully constructed composure crumble. A strange, hot flush crept up my neck. My jaw locked so tight I could feel the strain in my molars. Every instinct screamed at me to retreat, to melt into the shadows, but I was rooted. I forced myself to remain still, breathing shallowly, acutely aware of the pounding in my ears, waiting for the performance to end, waiting for the moment I could finally break free from this suffocating awareness.
